summary:
The Maglemosian culture was a Mesolithic hunter-gatherer-fisher tradition of northern Europe — principally Denmark, southern Sweden, northern Germany, and the now-submerged lowlands of [[event:1319|Doggerland]] — dated to approximately 9000–6000 BCE. The name derives from Maglemose ("Big Bog") in Denmark, where extensive waterlogged deposits preserved an unusual richness of organic artifacts normally lost to decay, providing an exceptionally detailed picture of early postglacial life in northwestern Europe.

Maglemosian communities exploited the productive lakes, rivers, bogs, and coastal zones of postglacial Scandinavia and the North Sea lowlands, fishing for pike and perch, hunting elk and wild boar, and gathering plant foods. Their technology included dugout canoes, fish weirs, nets made from plant fibers, bows and arrows with microlithic tips, and a wide variety of bone and antler tools. The care and artistry evident in carved and decorated bone tools and paddles demonstrate that Maglemosian communities invested in material culture beyond purely functional needs. The culture contributed to later Mesolithic traditions of Scandinavia such as the Ertebølle culture, which itself exhibited remarkable social complexity before the eventual adoption of farming.
